preamble: The shift from the high front rounded vowel [y] to its unrounded counterpart [i] is a process of delabialization (Unrounding). This is a common historical development as front rounded vowels are often phonetically unstable and tend to merge with their unrounded equivalents over time.

examples.note: The high front rounded vowels of Old English (resulting from i-mutation) unrounded during the transition to Middle English.
Greek iotacism involved the merger of several distinct vowels, including [y], into [i].
Yiddish systematically unrounded the front rounded vowels inherited from Middle High German.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
